Title: Polish president vetoes student rights, maritime bills in setback for government
Article:
WARSAW: Polish President Karol Nawrocki on Thursday vetoed two government-backed bills, rejecting legislation establishing school students' rights ombudsmen as "ideological" and blocking a maritime transport law that he said would implement elements of the European Union's Green Deal.
Announcing the decision, Nawrocki said the bill creating student rights ombudsmen would increase bureaucracy and politicise Poland's education system rather than strengthen protections for pupils, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
Instead of safeguarding students' rights, the legislation would create a "school prosecutor's office for pursuing anonymous denunciations", he wrote on the social media platform X, arguing it would lead to the ideological influence of schools.
The vetoes are another blow to Prime Minister Donald Tusk's governing coalition, which lacks the three-fifths parliamentary majority required to override a presidential veto.
Since taking office in 2025, Nawrocki has signalled that he will use his constitutional powers to block legislation that he believes conflicts with his conservative political agenda.
The student rights bill was intended to establish ombudsmen at the regional and national levels to help protect pupils' rights and provide an independent mechanism for handling complaints within the education system.
Supporters argued that it would strengthen legal safeguards for students, while opponents said it would create unnecessary bureaucracy and increase ideological influence in schools. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Viral video stirs debate over motorcycle parking habits [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A video showing
motorcycles parked haphazardly
in front of a restaurant has sparked debate online over the parking habits of motorcyclists in Malaysia.
The post, uploaded by Threads user @jokerasya, showed several motorcycles parked in an area not designated for them, despite proper motorcycle parking bays appearing to be available nearby.
"This is the main problem with Malaysians," the user wrote in the post, which included a video and a picture of the scene, believed to be in Shah Alam.
The post has since gained traction, drawing more than 4,000 likes and a stream of comments from social media users who said the incident reflected a wider problem of poor civic discipline.
Some users said the habit often spread quickly once one rider decided to park illegally or inconsiderately.
User @ijok_bundle summed it up, saying: "One does it, everyone follows. No enforcement. 'It's only for a while.' Because Malaysia."
Another user, @thurdotdesign, shared a similar experience, saying he was once teased by friends for parking his motorcycle at a proper motorcycle bay located slightly farther from a shop.
"When we entered the shop, everyone laughed at me and kept asking, 'Couldn't you have parked the motorcycle even farther?'
"From the start until we left, they kept repeating the same joke. I wasn't offended, just puzzled. Since when did doing the right thing become something to laugh at?" the user wrote.
Another user, @calvin_tan888, said many Malaysians disliked walking even short distances, adding that such an attitude had contributed to inconsiderate parking behaviour.
While many found the post amusing, others said the issue was no laughing matter calling for stricter enforcement, especially when motorcycles obstruct shopfronts, entrances or parking bays meant for other vehicles.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump Jr.'s gun retailer could gain from proposed home firearm delivery rule
Article:
WASHINGTON: Donald Trump Jr. helped take an online retailer known as the "Amazon of guns" public last year. Now, GrabAGun, where the US president's son is a shareholder and board member, could reap a windfall from a proposed rule change at Trump's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives that would make it easier to ship guns directly to people's homes.
If finalised, the rule would be among the most consequential changes to the US gun policy in two decades, potentially driving huge growth in online gun sales, according to ten industry officials, store owners, and gun-control advocates interviewed by Reuters.
The proposal would allow licensed dealers to ship firearms directly to in-state residents who undergo an online identity verification and background check, along with a seven-day waiting period after notifying local law enforcement. Currently, online buyers must pick up firearms at physical stores and undergo in-person background checks unless they have a permit.
Some gun shop owners, industry officials and gun control advocates argue the direct shipping of firearms poses significant public safety and security risks and threatens the viability of small brick-and-mortar gun shops. The change could benefit Trump Jr., whose more than 300,000 shares in GrabAGun are worth more than US$700,000 – down from more than US$5 million last year.
A spokesperson for Trump Jr., Andrew Surabian, said in a statement that the president's son had no role in the ATF proposal.
"Don is a lifelong businessman and vocal advocate of our Second Amendment rights," he said. "He does not interface with the Federal Government as part of his role with any company that he invests in or advises and had zero involvement in this particular decision."
GrabAGun CEO Marc Nemati told Reuters that neither he nor Trump Jr. knew this proposal was coming. He said in an interview that the company is still analysing the potential impact of the rule change on its US$100 million in revenue. The CEO was bullish, however, in a May news release: "We believe GrabAGun is uniquely positioned to capitalise on this potential opportunity."
A GROWTH MARKET IN FIREARMS HOME DELIVERY
The ATF projects that half of all gun buyers – nearly 3.3 million people a year – would eventually use the home-delivery method. Some industry leaders told Reuters they expect the actual numbers to be far higher because of the convenience of online shopping. Online ordering for in-store pickup currently represents a smaller but still substantial portion of US gun sales, industry experts say. ATF chief counsel Robert Leider said in an interview that he oversaw a team effort at the agency to craft the proposed rule. He said it aimed to align the gun industry with the rest of the modern economy. The agency estimates the change would save consumers US$103.7 million annually in travel and processing time.
Leider said he was unaware of Trump Jr.'s connection to GrabAGun until asked by Reuters and that the president's son had no influence on the proposed rule. Leider declined to say whether the White House had any role in the proposal.
The White House said it had no record or knowledge of any "interaction with the President's son on any of these topics." The rule is one of 34 deregulatory measures proposed by ATF this spring in response to a February 2025 presidential executive order to expand gun access.
Marianna Mitchem, a senior firearms industry advisor at Everytown for Gun Safety, worked at ATF for more than 20 years, including about four as the industry liaison. She said the industry had never asked for a similar policy in her interactions and that physical gun shops had historically played a key role in vetting owners.
"ATF always says the gun store is the first line of defence in gun safety," said Mitchem, who opposes the proposed rule. "But now they are flipping it."
'PEOPLE BUY EVERYTHING ONLINE'
Founded in 2010, GrabAGun is one of the nation's leading online-first gun retailers, though some competitors with physical retail operations also provide online ordering. GrabAGun would be poised for substantial growth under the new ATF rule but competitors including major outdoor stores and other retailers could also take advantage of the regulations, according to industry analysts, former ATF officials and gun shop owners.
Trump Jr. emerged as the face of GrabAGun last year when it went public through a special purpose acquisition company merger that netted the company US$119 million. That SPAC was brought by 1789 Capital, where Trump Jr. is a partner.
Shares of the company, which trades under the ticker PEW, have dropped 85% over the past year. Reuters could not establish the factors driving the stock's falling price.
Trump Jr. argued at the time that the online gun sales were safe because they were sending guns to gun shops for customer pickup – rather than directly to homes, as the new rule would allow. He also made TV appearances to argue that young people and women who may not otherwise want to visit a gun store would flock to the company's more accessible website. "People buy everything online," Trump Jr told Fox Business last summer.
GUN SHOPS WORRY ABOUT SAFETY, LOST BUSINESS
The proposed rule is now in a public comment period that closes in early August. The proposal may not be finalised until late 2026 or early 2027 and could still be withdrawn or changed.
Gun-control groups, including Everytown, Brady and Giffords, argue shipping millions of firearms to buyers' homes is ripe for illegal gun trafficking, mail theft and straw purchases, when a legal buyer purchases a firearm on behalf of someone prohibited from owning one.
"Even with the most robust virtual sales and background check process, there is no way for a gun store that is selling a gun over the internet to know if the person making the purchase is funneling the firearms to others," Giffords spokesperson Aneesa McMillan said in a statement.
The change coincides with a similar proposal by the US Postal Service to lift a century-old ban on mailing handguns.
Justin Anderson, the director of online sales at Hyatt Guns, which has a storefront in North Carolina and is one of the nation's largest gun retailers, said physical shops act as the "last line of defense" to ensure dangerous people do not obtain weapons. While Anderson said Hyatt would likely adopt home delivery if the rule is finalised, the safety concerns give him pause.
Smaller shop owners said in interviews they worried about both the safety and financial risks that would result from the proposal. Many smaller rural dealers survive on "transfer fees" – which can run about US$30 per weapon – charged to complete background checks for online purchases that require in-store delivery. Such transactions also drive foot traffic for ammunition and accessories that drive sales.
Chrystal Santos, who runs operations at Bow & Barrel Sportsmen Center in Missouri, submitted a public comment opposing the rule.
She said her staff is trained to spot straw purchases by reading customer behavior – an intuitive process she said online verification cannot replicate.
"It opens a whole can of worms," Santos said. "Places like GrabAGun and others are the problem, they make it harder for shops like us."
Leider defended the proposal, arguing that it calls for a verification system that is more secure than traditional retail.
"The people who are concerned about this being susceptible to straw purchases have an idealised view of what an in-store purchase is," Leider said, arguing that some physical shops act as "little more than a paperwork conduit."--REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fed seen less likely to raise rates as job growth slows
Article:
NEW YORK: Federal Reserve policymakers have less reason to raise interest rates later this month after a government report showed the US economy added far fewer jobs than expected over the past two months.
Nonfarm payrolls increased by 57,000 jobs in June, the Labour Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics said in its closely watched employment report on Thursday.
That was about half the increase economists had expected. May's job gains were revised down to 129,000 from the 172,000 initially reported.
"The slowdown in payroll growth challenges the narrative of renewed labour market strength that has been building in recent months but, importantly, reinforces the view that the Federal Reserve is under little pressure to tighten policy," wrote Principal Asset Management chief global strategist Seema Shah.
Traders in short-term interest rate futures now see less than a 20 per cent chance of a rate hike in July, although they continue to view a September increase as the more likely outcome.
Fed funds futures contracts imply about a 60 per cent chance of a September rate hike versus the policy rate remaining in the current range of 3.50 per cent to 3.75 per cent, down from about a 75 per cent probability before the jobs report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold gains more than 2pct after weak US payrolls report
Article:
NEW YORK: Gold rose more than two per cent on Thursday, extending gains after weaker-than-expected US nonfarm payrolls data reduced expectations of Federal Reserve interest rate hikes this year.
Spot gold rose 2.2 per cent to US$4,116.54 per ounce as of 1.20pm EDT (1720 GMT).
US gold futures settled 1.1 per cent higher at US$4,125.70.
The US dollar index fell 0.5 per cent, making US dollar-denominated metals cheaper for holders of other currencies.
"The lower-than-expected jobs number portends to less likelihood of potential rate hikes later this year. As we know, gold has a tendency to perform better in lower interest rate environments," said David Meger, director of metals trading at High Ridge Futures.
"Hence, we saw a significant rally in the gold market on the back of that," he added.
A US Labour Department report showed the economy added 57,000 jobs last month, compared with economists' estimates for an increase of 110,000. The unemployment rate stood at 4.2 per cent.
That followed a report on Wednesday showing US private payrolls increased less than expected in June.
Traders now see a nearly 51 per cent chance of a rate hike by September, down from 66 per cent before the data, according to the CME FedWatch tool.
On Wednesday, Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh said inflation expectations and inflation risks had eased in recent weeks, even as he reiterated that the Fed is committed to bringing inflation down to its two per cent target.
Meanwhile, the World Gold Council said central banks resumed buying in May and, based on the latest reported data, official gold reserves increased by a net 41 tonnes during the month.
On the Middle East front, Iran and the United States concluded a round of indirect talks on Wednesday with no sign they had made progress towards a lasting peace.
Among other precious metals, spot silver rose 2.6 per cent to US$60.69 per ounce, platinum gained 2.6 per cent to US$1,617 and palladium added 4.7 per cent to US$1,267.14.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cooling US jobs data buys the Fed and stock market more time
Article:
NEW YORK: A weaker-than-expected June jobs report has provided relief for US equities, easing concerns that a resilient labour market could force the US Federal Reserve to adopt a more hawkish stance on interest rates and weigh on technology stocks that have driven this year's market rally.
US job growth slowed more than expected in June, while payroll gains for the previous two months were revised lower, the Labour Department said on Thursday, pointing to a cooling but still stable labour market.
The data prompted financial markets to scale back expectations of a near-term Federal Reserve rate hike.
The report could buy the stock market more time as investors grapple with soaring valuations, sharp swings in trillion-dollar technology stocks and periodic sell-offs that have fuelled concerns some parts of the market may be in bubble territory.
US stocks initially edged higher before surrendering gains, while the US dollar weakened as traders pared expectations for a rate hike as early as September.
"This jobs report lets anyone concerned about an imminent Fed hike breathe a sigh of relief," said Adam Sarhan, chief executive of 50 Park Investments in New York.
"It doesn't mean the fear of inflation is over. It just takes the pressure off the Fed to raise rates in the short term," he said.
Last month, investors got a reminder of how quickly this year's 10 per cent stock market rally could unravel after the Fed left rates unchanged at its June meeting but projected a rate hike later this year amid concerns about persistent inflation.
That move heightened concerns over debt-funded corporate spending on artificial intelligence and triggered a pullback in stocks, particularly among large technology companies that have come to dominate the market.
Thursday's jobs report, which broke a run of strong employment gains in previous months, should reassure Fed policymakers that the labour market is not adding to inflationary pressures, investors said.
Fed funds futures late on Thursday indicated roughly even odds that the central bank would raise rates by its September meeting, according to LSEG data.
While many investors cautioned against drawing conclusions from a single report, particularly given recent data volatility, they said the figures give the Fed more time and should support equities, at least in the short term.
"A consistent pattern of moderating labour market conditions and easing inflation would reinforce the case for a more accommodative Fed and support the current market outlook," said Anshul Sharma, chief investment officer at Savvy Wealth.
The prospect of lower interest rates generally supports equity valuations, particularly in long-term growth sectors such as technology, he added.
FURTHER RE-PRICING IS POSSIBLE
Despite the modest pullback in expectations for a rate hike this year, there remains a gap between market pricing and the views of many economists, who believe the Fed will not raise rates this year.
That suggests there is scope for further market re-pricing.
Momentum and earnings expectations continue to be the primary drivers of the stock market rather than economic data.
Following a strong first-quarter earnings season for S&P 500 companies, investors will closely watch second-quarter results in the coming weeks to see whether they can continue to justify elevated valuations.
Strategists said that if expectations for a rate hike continue to diminish, it would provide an additional boost for equities.
"It certainly would shift towards more of a risk-on posture," said Mark Hackett, chief market strategist at Nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens firmer as weak US jobs data lifts rate cut hopes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened firmer against the US dollar on Friday after weaker-than-expected US jobs data for June reinforced expectations that the Federal Reserve (Fed) could adopt a more cautious approach to interest rates.
At 8.04am, the local currency rose to 4.0705/0805 against the greenback from Thursday's close of 4.0780/0830.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said US nonfarm payrolls increased by 57,000 in June, well below the consensus forecast of 114,000.
He said payroll figures for April and May were also revised lower, signalling that the labour market may not be strong enough to justify an immediate interest rate hike by the Fed.
"From a technical analysis perspective, the ringgit against the US dollar is already in oversold territory. This suggests the ringgit could appreciate further against the US dollar in the near term.
"Yesterday, the ringgit closed 0.33 per cent higher against the US dollar at RM4.0805. As such, we expect the US dollar/ringgit to hover between RM4.06 and RM4.07 today," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit was stronger against a basket of major currencies.
It appreciated against the British pound to 5.4296/4430 from 5.4401/4467 at Thursday's close, strengthened against the Japanese yen to 2.5214/5277 from 2.5295/5327, and edged up against the euro to 4.6497/6612 from 4.6526/6583.
The local currency also traded higher against regional currencies.
It rose against the Philippine peso to 6.61/6.63 from 6.62/6.63 previously, strengthened against the Indonesian rupiah to 226.2/226.8 from 226.6/226.9, advanced against the Thai baht to 12.2384/2744 from 12.2411/2613, and gained against the Singapore dollar to 3.1483/1563 from 3.1517/1558 at Thursday's close.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russian attacks kill two, injure 8 in Ukraine overnight, officials say
Article:
July 3 (Reuters) - Two people were killed and another eight injured in Russian overnight attacks on Ukraine, officials said on Telegram in the early hours of Friday.
In the bordering Sumy region, two were killed and one injured after Russian drones hit a private house, said Oleh Hryhorov, head of the regional military administration.
In the central Ukrainian city of Kryvyi Rih, hometown of President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, seven people were injured after a Russian missile strike on the densely built urban area, said Oleksandr Vilkul, the head of the city's defence council. A day of mourning will be observed in Kyiv on Friday after nearly three dozen people were killed there on Thursday in the deadliest Russian attack on the Ukrainian capital this year. (Reporting by Jekaterīna Golubkova in Tokyo; Editing by Stephen Coates)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Do not open until 2276: US burying time capsule to mark July 4
Article:
WASHINGTON: Americans living in 2276 will get a taste of what their ancestors today think best represents the nation thanks to a time capsule to be buried this July 4.
The 400kg stainless steel cylinder will go into the ground Saturday, as the nation marks its 250th birthday, not far from where the Declaration of Independence was signed in Philadelphia.
It is not supposed to be opened for another 250 years, in 2276.
But it is not the only such gift for US people far off in the future. Another time capsule scheduled for opening when the United States turns 500 years old was unveiled a few days ago in Washington.
The contents of the Washington one are not widely known but the capsule due to be buried in Philadelphia near Independence Hall has spilled all its secrets.
All 50 states, five US territories such as Puerto Rico, plus an array of sporting and cultural organizations have made contributions to this collective national snapshot.
Together it all makes for "a representative record of the United States at 250 years," said Rosie Rios, chairwoman of America250, the official organization behind the national commemoration of the anniversary.
- EAGLE FEATHER AND AI -
Wisconsin, for instance, has contributed a brown feather. It comes from a bald eagle known as "Old Abe" – named for president Abraham Lincoln – that served as a mascot to Union troops in over 30 battles in the US Civil War.
Ohio has offered a piece of fabric from the wings of the airplane built by Wilbur and Orville Wright, the pioneers who made aviation history in 1903 with the first powered, sustained flight.
From Maine comes a bone from the endangered North Atlantic right whale.
Other states opted for the stuff of modern life, such as California, which offers the response from the AI chatbot Claude to this prompt: "Write me a prediction of what California will be like 250 years from July 4, 2026."
America250 itself has chipped in a late-model orange iPhone.
Other pieces of Americana that made the cut: a bookmark made by the Wabanaki, a Native American tribe; a diamond from Arkansas; a recipe for anis-flavored cookies from New Mexico; and an Oklahoma City Thunder pin from when the team won the NBA basketball championship in 2025.
- ENDURING UNDERGROUND -
Other ideas were discarded, like a leather American football, because of doubts it would endure 250 years underground.
Jay Nanninga, a National Institute of Standards and Technology mechanical engineer who designed the time capsule, had to address such storage issues. It was decided to bury the objects rather than insert them in a statue.
He considered different shapes for the container – like a square or even a star – but thought it best to avoid having seams and joints, so a stainless steel cylinder won the day.
The cylinder will be placed inside a metallic bell that leaves an air pocket that is supposed to keep moisture out of the inner chamber.
The cylinder itself is sealed with a thin protective layer of a soft, malleable metal called indium.
Papers destined to be read in 250 years are stored in a separate compartment inside the cylinder and sealed, while the other objects were placed in cardboard boxes.
Nanninga said engineers have watched how stainless steel aged over the 100 years it has been in use, and he thinks the material will hold up well in this national exercise of self-storage.
"I do think in 250 years all the stainless will be in really good shape," he said.
America has done this kind of thing before.
A time capsule buried in 1876 was opened in 1976.
Another capsule, created for that year's bicentennial, is stored at the National Archives, to be opened in 2076, said Rios.
She said when the new capsule buried in Philadelphia is finally opened in 2276, "we want future generations to have a clear, authentic window into who we were at 250."
"What we valued, what we built, and how we saw ourselves as a nation," she added.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MOSTI to prioritise talent development ahead of AMMSTI-23
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (MOSTI) will place talent development at the forefront of its agenda as Malaysia prepares to host the 23rd Asean Ministerial Meeting on Science, Technology and Innovation (AMMSTI-23) next year.
Its minister, Datuk Chang Lih Kang, said talent development remains a key priority as it is critical to supporting the country's efforts to attract more high-tech investments.
"Besides talent development, our focus areas include energy transition, Artificial Intelligence (AI), digitalisation, advanced materials, nanotechnology, including hydrogen, and biotechnology," he said.
He told reporters this after the MOSTI TechTalks Series 2/2026 programme, titled
AI and the Future of Engineering Careers
, at the Tun Dr Ismail Library, Universiti Teknologi MARA (UiTM) Johor Branch, Pasir Gudang Campus, here last night.
Malaysia is scheduled to host AMMSTI-23 in June 2027, following the decision made at the 22nd ASEAN Ministerial Meeting on Science, Technology and Innovation (AMMSTI-22) in Vientiane, Laos, on June 26.
In another development, Chang said MOSTI remains committed to supporting other ministries in strengthening Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) programmes to better meet current industry demands, although TVET does not fall under the ministry's direct jurisdiction.
He said TVET curricula should be enhanced with emerging technologies such as robotics, artificial intelligence and coding, rather than focusing solely on conventional technical skills.
According to him, the initiative involves collaboration with 12 ministries involved in TVET implementation, including the Ministry of Education, the Ministry of Higher Education, the Ministry of Rural and Regional Development, and the Ministry of Human Resources.
On the MOSTI TechTalks initiative, Chang said the programme, which is held regularly at university campuses, serves as a platform to engage young people and expose them to the latest developments in Malaysia's science, technology and innovation (STI) ecosystem.
He said such engagements are important to ensure university students understand the direction of the country's STI agenda and are better prepared to meet the demands of the future workforce. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dollar slides after soft jobs report, yen surges
Article:
NEW YORK: The US dollar fell sharply on Thursday after the closely watched June employment report showed US employers added far fewer jobs than expected, while the Japanese yen surged as traders braced for possible intervention by Japanese authorities.
Employers added 57,000 jobs, below economists' expectations for 110,000 job gains. The unemployment rate fell to 4.2 per cent from 4.3 per cent.
Markets quickly repriced their expectations for US Federal Reserve policy. Fed funds futures traders now see a 54 per cent chance of a rate hike by September, down from 67 per cent before the report.
"It is weaker than expected, but the bulk of the numbers were in leisure and hospitality. That's probably driven more by seasonal factors than by anything else, so it's not very nefarious," Sarah Ying, head of FX strategy at CIBC Capital Markets, said.
The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, was last down 0.56 per cent at 100.83.
It earlier reached 100.55, the lowest since June 18, and was headed for its biggest one-day drop since April 30.
The euro gained 0.52 per cent to US$1.1435 and reached US$1.1472, the highest since June 22.
The dollar had been buoyed in recent months by rising expectations that the Fed would raise rates as it continued to battle inflation running well above its two per cent annual target.
Strong capital inflows tied to the artificial intelligence boom have also supported the currency.
Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh said on Wednesday he would stick firmly to the central bank's two per cent inflation target, but noted that inflation expectations and inflation risks had eased in recent weeks.
"Unless we continue to see disappointments in the labour market data, it still feels like the AI narrative is driving a lot of the flow," Ying said.
YEN SURGES
The Japanese yen rallied sharply against the US dollar on Thursday as traders weighed a shift in intervention strategy by Japan's Ministry of Finance and speculated whether Tokyo had already stepped into the market.
Sources told Reuters Japanese officials were abandoning their practice of telegraphing intervention risks, instead signalling a more targeted campaign to squeeze speculators and raise the cost of betting against the yen.
Officials were also avoiding any suggestion of a specific "line in the sand" exchange rate that would trigger action, adopting a more aggressive approach aimed at keeping traders guessing.
"If they're not going to give guidance, the MOF (Ministry of Finance) can come in at any time. So that is a scarier thought, I would think, relative to the current status quo," Ying said.
"It's just a more aggressive way for the MOF to communicate and to respond to yen weakness."
The Japanese yen gained 0.95 per cent against the US dollar to 161.04 per dollar and reached 160.62, its strongest level since June 18. It is on track for its biggest one-day gain against the US dollar since April 30.
What triggered the move remained unclear, and Japan's Ministry of Finance declined to comment.
Traders and strategists offered differing explanations, with some speculating that authorities had checked rates in the market — a move that typically signals a willingness to intervene and can rattle currency markets on its own.
"We will have to wait for data to ascertain if this was intervention, but the timing of the move does suggest that it was," Abbas Keshvani, Asia macro strategist at RBC Capital Markets in Singapore, said.
In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin gained 2.8 per cent to US$61,762.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dow jumps to record closing high after soft US jobs data, Nasdaq down with chip shares
Article:
NEW YORK: The Dow rose more than one per cent to a record closing high on Thursday ahead of the long holiday weekend as a softer-than-expected US jobs report eased worries about interest rate hikes, while another sharp drop in chipmaker stocks weighed on the Nasdaq.
The S&P 500 ended flat on the day.
The Dow recorded a fourth straight week of gains, its longest such streak since October 2024.
The US market will be closed on Friday in observance of the US Independence Day holiday.
The US nonfarm payrolls report showed the economy added 57,000 jobs last month, far below economists' estimates for a rise of 110,000.
The unemployment rate was 4.2 per cent, in line with expectations of 4.3 per cent.
The employment report followed a run of strong job gains recently.
Expectations for a rate hike from the Federal Reserve decreased after the report, according to CME FedWatch.
For the September meeting, hike expectations dimmed to 55 per cent from 64.1 per cent.
The jobs report "doesn't mean the fear of inflation is over," said Adam Sarhan, chief executive at 50 Park Investments in New York.
"It just takes the pressure off the Fed to raise rates in the short term."
Investors have been worried about inflation, especially given sharp gains in oil prices at the start of the Iran war.
Apple shares rose 4.8 per cent and helped support all three major indexes. Nikkei Asia reported that Apple plans to launch five new iPhone models.
On the flip side, an index of semiconductors ended 5.4 per cent lower on Thursday, falling sharply for a second day. Shares of Nvidia fell 1.4 per cent while SanDisk dropped 14.1 per cent.
Investors are likely taking profits in chip stocks following this year's strong gains, said Bruce Zaro, managing director at Granite Wealth Management in Plymouth, Massachusetts.
The semiconductor index remains up about 78 per cent for the year to date.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 594.83 points, or 1.14 per cent, to 52,900.07, the S&P 500 gained 0.01 points to 7,483.24 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 207.36 points, or 0.80 per cent, to 25,832.67.
For the week, the Dow rose about two per cent, the S&P 500 gained 1.8 per cent and the Nasdaq advanced 2.1 per cent.
Tesla shares dropped 7.5 per cent even though the electric carmaker posted second-quarter deliveries above estimates. Tesla shares had risen sharply this week ahead of the report.
Among other decliners, Bending Spoons dropped 11.3 per cent, a day after the Vimeo owner gained 40 per cent in its debut on the Nasdaq.
Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.42-to-one ratio on the NYSE. There were 318 new highs and 111 new lows on the NYSE.
On the Nasdaq, 2,419 stocks rose and 2,548 fell as declining issues outnumbered advancers by a 1.05-to-one ratio.
The S&P 500 posted no new 52-week highs and no new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded no new highs and no new lows.
Volume on US exchanges was 19.92 billion shares, compared with the 23.34 billion average for the full session over the last 20 trading days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Algerians vote for new parliament amid public apathy
Article:
ALGIERS, (Algeria ): Algerians voted to elect a new parliament in a ballot yesterday marked by apathy and the disqualification of several hopefuls.
About 25 million eligible voters are choosing 407 lawmakers in the lower house of parliament from 1,235 candidates amid cost-of-living concerns and shrinking freedoms, according to rights groups.
Polling stations, which opened at 8:00 am local time (0700 GMT) and closed at 8:00 pm, were nearly empty in the capital Algiers as of early afternoon, an AFP journalist reported.
But images broadcast on national television showed people lining up to cast their ballots in some parts of the country, including in the northwestern province of El Aricha.
Provisional results were due within 48 hours.
Voter turnout had reached 11 percent nationwide as of mid-afternoon, and nearly 10 per cent among those registered to cast ballots overseas, state TV reported, citing the election authority.
"We came to fulfil our duty, hoping for something good. That's all I have to say," Djammel Bouakkaz, a 74-year-old retiree, told AFP at a polling station in Algiers.
The last parliamentary vote in 2021 saw a historic low turnout, while this year's is taking place amid controversy over the government's disqualification of roughly a third of would-be candidates.
Some hopefuls have said they were blocked from competing for major constituencies, including Algiers.
The ruling National Liberation Movement (FLN) was widely expected to still dominate the 407-seat National People's Assembly, whose members are elected for five-year terms.
Interior Minister Said Sayoud declined to comment on turnout but earlier urged citizens to take part, according to news website TSA.
Algeria had seen record low turnout of 23 per cent in the 2021 poll — the first since a major pro-democracy protest movement swept through the country in 2019.
The Hirak movement erupted in February of that year and led to the resignation of long-serving president Abdelaziz Bouteflika two months later.
President Abdelmajid Tebboune was elected later in 2019 and won a second term in 2024.
"Throughout this campaign, the crucial challenge has been to encourage citizens to turn out in large numbers," wrote the local French-language daily L'Expression. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Resilient Bursa in June sets stage for second half upside [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia showed resilience through June's market volatility, supported by a strong domestic institutional investor base that helped cushion the market against external shocks.
Despite a brief mid-month tech selloff, sentiment remained steady as early signs of renewed interest emerge, according to analysts.
This sets the stage for a bullish second half of 2026 with JP Morgan keeping an "overweight" stance on Malaysian equities for the next six months.
JP Morgan cited a resilient domestic investment cycle, supportive positioning and attractive banking sector fundamentals for its bullish outlook for the second half.
JP Morgan equity strategists Yen Voo and Khoi Vu said Malaysia's outlook remains supported despite a firmer US dollar and continued foreign selling pressure, with domestic liquidity and earnings resilience expected to anchor performance into the second half of 2026.
The US investment bank maintains its 1,800 end-2026 FBM KLCI target, underpinned by seven per cent earnings growth and 14x forward price to earnings.
Rakuten Trade vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng said Malaysia remains largely a "captive market", where local institutional investors dominate shareholding and help stabilise the benchmark index during volatility.
Technology stocks experienced heightened volatility in June as a global technology sell-off and escalating geopolitical tensions rattled investor sentiment, but the sector remained among Bursa Malaysia's strongest performers.
The Bursa Malaysia Technology Index came under pressure on June 8 following a sharp decline in US technology stocks and renewed tensions in the Middle East after fresh missile strikes by Iran.
It came under renewed selling pressure on June 23 after a sell-off in South Korean technology shares spilled over to regional markets, dragging the index down nearly four per cent intraday.
Despite the setbacks, the technology index ended June 1.35 per cent higher at 74.58 points from 73.87 points at the end of May, outperforming the FBM KLCI, which declined 1.13 per cent over the same period.
The technology index has also surged about 46 per cent year-on-year from 51.10 points at the end of June 2025.
On the recent rebound in technology stocks, Thong said valuations in major markets had become stretched, suggesting the sector could be entering a period of consolidation rather than extending its rally.
"Artificial intelligence (AI) and related stocks are overbought in the US, Japan and South Korea.
"We believe they are entering a consolidation period," he said.
Looking ahead to the second half of 2026, Thong expects plantation and construction stocks to remain among the market's leading sectors.
This will be supported by stronger crude palm oil prices and improving construction order books.
Trade view Capital fund manager Neoh Jia Man said the FBM KLCI's resilience during the technology sector volatility was largely due to the benchmark index's limited exposure to technology stocks.
"Nevertheless, Bursa Malaysia continues to lag behind other regional markets with similarly low technology exposure, including the Philippines, Thailand and Singapore," he said.
Neoh noted that foreign investors remained net sellers in June, recording net outflows of nearly RM2.4 billion.
"We believe foreign investor interest in the Malaysian market is likely to remain subdued due to concerns over a more hawkish monetary policy stance by the Federal Reserve, as well as uncertainty surrounding the possibility of an early general election.
"While we do not have visibility into the Employees Provident Fund's (EPF) positioning, local institutional investors have been net buyers over the past two months," he said.
Neoh said there were signs of renewed confidence in the AI and data centre investment theme following the temporary truce in the Middle East, coupled with strong earnings guidance from industry bellwethers.
"That said, valuation concerns are unlikely to dissipate anytime soon, given the sharp rally in AI-related stocks," he said.
Neoh believes the plantation and construction sectors have the potential to perform well in the second half of 2026.
He said although easing energy prices could dampen fuel substitution demand and, in turn, weigh on crude palm oil (CPO) prices, the increasing likelihood of a Super El Niño later this year could support palm oil prices and benefit plantation companies.
For the construction sector, he said higher logistics and building material costs could delay contract awards and compress margins.
"However, demand for data centre-related construction remains robust, and we continue to see upside from the rollout of additional infrastructure projects under Budget 2027," he added.
In a trader's brief yesterday, Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) said the FBM KLCI's technical outlook had turned bearish after the benchmark closed at a seven-month low of 1,656.8 on July 1.
The firm said the index confirmed a double-top reversal after breaking below its rising trendline near 1,730 and the key 200-day moving average (MA200) of 1,676, with weakening momentum indicators reinforcing the downside bias.
It said the index must decisively reclaim 1,676, 1,685 (MA20) and 1,706 (MA50) to avoid further downside risks towards 1,650, 1,638 (MA280) and 1,625, representing the 38.2 per cent Fibonacci retracement level.
"A sustained break above 1,706 is needed to restore the medium-term uptrend towards 1,733.
"After a cautious debut in July, the KLCI is expected to remain choppy and headline-driven, with sentiment dictated by developments in US-Iran diplomacy and hawkish Fed expectations weighing on the ringgit and trade flows.
"Domestically, persistent foreign selling, a rising political risk premium amid the possibility of an early GE16, and the proposed expansion of the FBM KLCI from 30 to 50 constituents to broaden market representation and enhance sector diversification are likely to keep the benchmark in an extended consolidation phase," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Google loses fight against record €4.1 billion EU antitrust fine
Article:
BRUSSELS: Alphabet's Google on Thursday lost its long-running fight against a record EU antitrust fine for using its Android mobile operating system to block rivals, a court ruling seen as likely to boost Europe's crackdown on Big Tech.
The European Commission, the bloc's antitrust regulator, has fined Google billions of euros over several antitrust violations in the last 15 years, but the cases dragged through the courts as the search giant filed appeals.
The Android fine was imposed in 2018, a year after the Commission fined Google €2.42 billion (US$2.77 billion) for using its shopping comparison service to gain an unfair advantage over smaller rivals. Google lost an appeal against that in 2021.
The appeal in the Android case lasted longer.
The Commission fined Google €4.34 billion for agreements that forced phone manufacturers to pre-install Google Search, the Chrome browser and the Google Play app store on their Android devices, while preventing them from using rival operating systems.
A lower tribunal subsequently trimmed the fine to €4.1 billion in 2022. Google then appealed to the Luxembourg-based Court of Justice of the European Union, Europe's highest court.
The court sided with the EU antitrust enforcer.
"The appeal brought by Google and its parent company Alphabet against the judgment of the General Court is dismissed, thereby confirming the penalty imposed for Google Search's abuse of a dominant position in the context of the Android operating system," the judges said.
A Google spokesperson said the judgment failed to take into account the company's investment to ensure Android remains open, interoperable and free.
"In any event, we adapted our agreements to comply with the initial decision back in 2018, and we remain focused on continued innovation and openness for our users, partners and developers," Google said.
Google has racked up close to €11 billion in EU fines in the last decade for various antitrust infringements.
While the record antitrust fine is less than three per cent of Alphabet's annual profit, the ruling could embolden other regulators and companies to pursue Google for damages.
The loss in the shopping comparison case has already led to a slew of lawsuits against Google by companies seeking damages worth billions of US dollars across half a dozen countries.
A Swedish court on Wednesday ordered Google to pay about US$1.5 billion in damages to PriceRunner, the price comparison business now owned by Klarna.
Google could also face more fines in the near future for allegedly favouring its own services and products in search results and for practices related to its app store, both of which fall under the Digital Markets Act aimed at curbing the power of Big Tech.
The case is C-738/22 P Google and Alphabet v Commission.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PublicInvest sees brent easing to US$70–US$85 as Iran risk premium fades
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Brent crude is expected to trade in a softer range of US$70 TO US$85 per barrel in the second half of 2026, as geopolitical tensions ease following a US–Iran interim agreement to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
Public Investment Bank Bhd (PublicInvest) said the agreement, reached after 111 days of conflict, has triggered a sharp unwinding of the earlier war risk premium, with Brent retreating from an intraday high of US$126 per barrel to around US$72 as disrupted barrels gradually return to the global market.
It noted that the de-escalation came earlier than its previous base case of a five-month escalation cycle outlined in March 2026, driven partly by physical market tightness that pushed refiners to secure alternative crude supplies and accelerate inventory drawdowns.
PublicInvest said it now expects Brent to average US$83 per barrel in 2026, revising its earlier assumptions to reflect a strong first half price spike followed by a lower trading range in the second half of 2026.
However, it added that prices are unlikely to return to pre-conflict lows due to a gradual normalisation of tanker flows, ongoing restoration of selected energy facilities, and the need to rebuild inventories.
The firm also highlighted that the political backdrop may have influenced the timing of the agreement, ahead of the US mid-term elections in November 2026, as elevated fuel prices have become a key cost-of-living concern.
US retail gasoline prices averaged US$4.6 per gallon in May 2026, about 45 per cent above pre-war levels and close to the 2022 highs during the Russia–Ukraine conflict.
On sector outlook, PublicInvest maintained an "Overweight" call on the oil and gas sector, although it lowered target prices for selected stocks following revisions to earnings assumptions.
The firm maintained an "Outperform" call on Hibiscus Petroleum Bhd with a revised target price of RM2.80, down from RM3.35, while Dialog Group Bhd was kept at "Neutral" with a lower target price of RM2.07.
The firm said the revised Brent outlook is negative versus its earlier war-premium assumptions but still supportive for upstream players within the US$70–85 per barrel range.
It continues to favour Hibiscus Petroleum, citing its direct leverage to realised oil prices, strong cash flow generation, and production growth from Brunei MLJ LPC, UK Teal West and North Sabah SF30, which keeps the group on track to achieve its 35,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day production target.
PublicInvest also cut its financial year 2027 and 2028 (FY27 and FY28) earnings forecasts by 29 per cent and 37 per cent, respectively.
It said that while FY26 earnings remain unchanged, most of Hibiscus' fourth-quarter 2026 oil and condensate offtake has already been sold at around US$120 per barrel.
This reflects the removal of earlier high-price assumptions following the revision to a lower Brent crude outlook.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tropicana taps hospitality for recurring income, partners Accor for Genting project
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tropicana Corp Bhd is expanding beyond township development into hospitality as it seeks to build a more resilient earnings base through recurring income and asset management.
The property company has partnered with Accor Group to launch Mercure Living Genting Highlands, billed as Malaysia's first Accor-branded condotel, at its Tropicana Grandhill development in Pahang.
The RM1.06 billion project, slated for completion in 2028, will comprise 1,443 fully furnished units within the group's Tropicana WindCity township.
Tropicana said the development has achieved an 88 per cent take-up rate and is about 30 per cent complete. Under the partnership, Accor will manage hotel operations, rental programmes and international marketing.
Its chief executive officer Dennis Ng said the move forms part of the company's strategy to reduce its reliance on traditional property sales by integrating hospitality into selected developments to generate sustainable recurring income.
"As the market continues to evolve, we are equally evolving our business model to create a stronger and more balanced platform for long-term growth," he said.
"Our objective is to complement our traditional development business with sustainable recurring income streams that enhance shareholder value while strengthening the competitiveness and resilience of our townships," he said.
Ng said the collaboration with Accor demonstrates how global hospitality partnerships can unlock new value from the company's developments and create professionally managed lifestyle assets that benefit homeowners, investors and the wider community.
He said Tropicana is also evaluating similar hospitality-led developments across its portfolio, including Tropicana WindCity in Genting Highlands, Lido Waterfront Boulevard in Johor, Tropicana Cenang in Langkawi and other future resort-oriented projects.
Accor said the launch of Mercure Living Genting Highlands supports its expansion of extended-stay accommodation in Malaysia.
Chief operating officer for Accor's premium, midscale and economy division in Asia, Garth Simmons, said travellers are increasingly seeking accommodation that combines flexibility, comfort and local experiences.
"We believe this development will be well positioned to meet those needs while contributing to the continued growth of Genting Highlands as a leisure destination," he said.
Accor's chief development officer for Asia, Andrew Langdon, said Genting Highlands continues to attract rising visitor numbers from both domestic and international markets, providing favourable long-term prospects for hospitality investments.
He added that Mercure Living Genting Highlands is expected to become the largest Mercure Living development globally.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AirAsia poised for comeback as lower oil prices support expansion plans, says Fernandes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: AirAsia Group is preparing for a major expansion, with plans to restore lower airfares, add new routes and improve connectivity as easing oil prices and improving market conditions bolster the airline's recovery, Capital A Bhd chief executive officer Tan Sri Tony Fernandes said.
In a social media post, Fernandes said the airline had emerged stronger after navigating the challenges brought about by the Covid-19 pandemic, crediting long-term business partners for supporting the carrier through one of the most difficult periods in its history.
Fernandes cited AirAsia's longstanding relationship with GE Aerospace, recalling a recent visit to the company's headquarters in Cincinnati.
He said GE Aerospace had backed AirAsia from its early days by supplying engines when the airline was still building its business, describing the relationship as one founded on trust and mutual commitment.
When we started AirAsia, they took a leap of faith with their engine. When we bought more than 1,000 of their engines, they gifted us with one free engine, which still sits at the entrance of AirAsia's headquarters," he said.
Fernandes said the experience reinforced a key lesson from the pandemic – the importance of partners who remain committed during difficult times rather than focusing on short-term challenges.
"One of the lessons I learned from Covid is to know who your friends are. Friends and partners will stand by you through thick and thin and know that the turbulent times too shall pass... Anyone can write a headline, but true partnerships are built on years of shared struggle and mutual respect, and speculative stories don't have a place in between," he said.
"That's what a true partnership and trust look like. That's what true friendship means in business. You don't build an airline empire alone; you build it with people who believe in your resilience and the ability to get back on your feet, even when things look not so rosy," he said.
AirAsia has been rebuilding its operations following the pandemic, restoring capacity, expanding its fleet and increasing regional connectivity as travel demand across Asia continues to recover.
Looking ahead, Fernandes said the easing of geopolitical tensions and softer oil prices provide a more supportive operating environment for the airline.
"The war has ended (hopefully for good), oil prices are coming down, and AirAsia is ready for a massive comeback story," Fernandes said.
He added that customers can expect lower fares, an expanded route network and greater connectivity as the airline accelerates its growth plans.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's healthcare sector enters high-growth phase [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's healthcare sector is entering a high-growth phase, with the industry projected to expand by about 12 per cent by 2030, according to Frost & Sullivan.
This will be driven by an ageing population, rising non-communicable diseases (NCDs), private sector expansion and accelerating digital health adoption, its head of healthcare and life sciences for Asia Pacific Rathanesh Ramasundram said.
Rathanesh said the sector is underpinned by strong structural demand drivers and is expected to grow to around RM158 billion by 2029, from RM74 billion in 2022, representing an annual growth rate of 11.4 per cent.
"We are seeing very strong structural forces supporting this expansion," she said at the Bursa-HLIB 21st Stratum Focus Series: Malaysia's Healthcare Industry Moving Forward forum today.
Ageing Population To Drive Healthcare Demand
Rathanesh said Malaysia is on track to become an ageing nation, with around 15 per cent of the population aged 60 and above by 2030, increasing to 21 per cent by 2044.
The demographic shift is expected to significantly raise demand for chronic disease management and long-term care.
"Cardiovascular disease, cancer and diabetes remain the dominant healthcare challenges in Malaysia. These conditions cost about RM64.2 billion annually or 4.2 per cent of GDP."
Private Healthcare, Medical Tourism Gain Momentum
Malaysia's private healthcare sector, comprising around 290 private hospitals, is expanding rapidly amid rising insurance penetration and growing middle-class demand.
Medical and health insurance and takaful claims reached RM6.75 billion in 2023, a six-fold increase since 2003.
Malaysia also welcomed 1.6 million healthcare travellers in 2024, generating RM2.4 billion in revenue.
"We are positioned well in orthopaedics, cardiology and oncology, and our key advantage is affordability combined with quality care," Rathanesh said.
She noted that preventive care, chronic disease management and digital transformation will shape the next phase of healthcare growth in Malaysia.
Weak Referral System Leads To Cost Pressures
Association of Private Hospitals Malaysia (APHM) said the country's healthcare system is facing a structural weakness in primary care integration.
APHM honorary secretary Anwar Anis said general practitioners (GPs) are not being effectively utilised as gatekeepers, resulting in unnecessary hospital visits and higher healthcare costs.
The weak primary care referral system is contributing to rising demand and cost pressures across the healthcare sector, he added.
Many patients bypass GP clinics and head straight to hospitals for conditions that could be treated at primary care level, resulting in avoidable congestion.
"This contributes to overutilisation in the system because many cases that reach hospitals could actually be managed by GPs," he said.
Anwar said the World Bank findings support the view that rising healthcare costs are driven mainly by higher utilisation rather than treatment price increases.
He said the data shows utilisation growth is higher than earlier estimates and makes up most of medical inflation.
He also cited ageing populations and rising NCDs as key drivers of increased hospital visits.
"When we talk about a 15 per cent increase, about 60-70 per cent of that is actually driven by utilisation. This means medical inflation is often misunderstood, as it is not mainly caused by higher procedure costs.
"It is not that the price of treatment has increased by 15 per cent. The main factor is more patients coming in due to ageing and NCDs," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Giant century-old tree crashes onto homes, traps resident
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A giant tree, believed to be more than a century old, crashed onto two houses at Jalan Batu Gantung here last night, trapping one resident and damaging properties.
The tree, estimated to weigh tens of tonnes, uprooted at about 7.53pm, damaging gates, roofs, a car porch and three vehicles, including a Proton X50, a Toyota Vios and a Nissan Almera.
Paya Terubong Fire and Rescue Station operations supervisor Mohd Zaini Zainudin said firefighters received an emergency call at 8.48pm.
He said when they arrived, they found a resident trapped inside one of the houses, not due to injuries but because the fallen tree had blocked the only exit.
"The victim was safely assisted after firefighters cut and cleared the branches obstructing the house. No injuries were reported.
"The operation was further complicated by fallen electrical cables and massive tree trunks and branches, which posed a risk to rescue personnel. We also had to ensure the tree did not shift during cutting works," he said when met at the scene.
Zaini said the operation involved 19 firefighters and two members of the Fire and Rescue Department's Emergency Medical Rescue Services (EMRS), with assistance from the Civil Defence Force (CDF), volunteer fire brigades from Kampung Pisang, Bukit Bendera and Lebuh Bakau, as well as the Penang Island City Council (MBPP).
He said one house sustained minor damage to its gate and roof and was unoccupied, while the second house suffered damage to its gate, car porch roof and one vehicle.
He said firefighters had cleared access to the trapped resident's home, while MBPP would require a crane to remove the remaining large branches.
Amerjit Kaur, 82, whose husband was trapped inside their house, said the incident could have turned fatal as they usually spent their evenings together in the garden.
However, that evening, Amerjit was out for dinner with her daughter, Parveen Sidhu, 49, and her family, who were back for a short visit from Kuala Lumpur.
Amerjit said she received a call from her husband at about 7.52pm while she was having dinner, informing her that the tree had crashed onto their property.
"I told him to remain inside the house and alerted the Fire and Rescue Department before rushing home. When I arrived, the firefighters were already there.
"The whole garden was gone. The car porch, fencing and gate were destroyed, while our cars were damaged. Even the garden chairs and plants were crushed," she said when met.
Amerjit said she and her husband would normally sit or stroll in the garden every evening, but they had been spared because she was out and her husband had remained inside the house when the tree fell.
She claimed residents had repeatedly raised concerns over the ageing tree with the authorities, urging that it be removed or properly managed, but no action was taken.
"By God's grace, we are still alive. If both of us had been sitting outside as usual, we might not have survived," she added.
She urged the state government and city council to take responsibility for the damage, saying the authorities should compensate affected residents and act more decisively on hazardous trees to prevent similar incidents in future.
She also expressed disappointment that no elected representative had visited the scene while emergency responders were carrying out rescue and clearing operations.
Her daughter, Parveen Sidhu, asked what would have happened if she had not been home.
"If I wasn't back this week, I don't know who would have helped them. My mother is in her 80s and she is devastated," she said.
Parveen questioned why stronger preventive action had not been taken despite tree maintenance being carried out periodically.
She urged MBPP to repair the damaged property promptly, saying her elderly parents could not safely remain in the house until repairs were completed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russia strikes Kyiv, killing 27, as both sides promise escalation
Article:
KYIV: Ukraine and Russia on Thursday vowed fresh assaults in their over-four-year war after Moscow launched a massive barrage on Kyiv, killing at least 27 people, tearing open apartment buildings and sending tens of thousands to shelters.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said his forces would "definitely" retaliate for the overnight pummelling as he inspected an apartment block that was partially destroyed.
The European Union's top diplomat proposed new sanctions on Moscow, as Zelenskyy pressed the United States for licences to manufacture Patriot air-defence missiles.
UN chief Secretary-General Antonio Guterres condemned Russia's barrage and repeated a ceasefire call.
"Attacks against civilians and civilian infrastructure wherever they occur are a clear violation of international humanitarian law and must stop immediately," said his spokesman Stephane Dujarric.
But the Kremlin vowed to further ramp up "pressure" on Kyiv, sticking to its no-compromise rhetoric.
- 'MOST MASSIVE ATTACK' -
Russia has routinely launched waves of missiles and drones during its invasion, Europe's deadliest conflict since World War II.
AFP journalists in central and eastern Kyiv heard more than a dozen explosions and saw residents rushing to shelter in metro stations.
In the morning, locals stood on the rubble of destroyed apartment blocks ripped apart by the barrage, as smoke poured over the Kyiv skyline.
Blasts started echoing out late on Wednesday, lasting into the early hours of Thursday as Russian missiles and drones rained down on residential areas in the city centre.
Mayor Vitali Klitschko described it as the "enemy's most massive attack on the capital."
The head of the city's military administration Tymur Tkachenko said 27 people had been killed and 91 wounded.
The Ukrainian branch of the Red Cross said its key warehouse had been destroyed and around US$2 million worth of humanitarian aid lost.
Debris also damaged a building "hosting a number of diplomats", EU spokeswoman Anitta Hipper told AFP, adding that the EU diplomats were safe.
And in Kryvyi Rig, two people were wounded when a missile struck a densely populated area, the head of the central city's military administration said early Friday.
Kyiv urged its allies to send more air defence.
"We also very much count on a decision by the United States regarding licences for Patriots," Zelenskyy said in a post on Facebook on Thursday.
Later, after talks with Finland's President Alexander Stubb, he added: "Defending against ballistic missiles is impossible without a sufficient number of missiles for Patriot systems."
Ukraine wants to manufacture munitions for the US-made missile interceptor system, but defence experts say it would take time to set up production domestically.
A US official told AFP President Donald Trump wanted a peace deal to end the "senseless killing" in Ukraine.
- STRUGGLING TO SLEEP -
Russia fired 496 drones and 74 missiles, including hard-to-intercept ballistic projectiles, Ukraine's air force said.
It said it shot down 48 of the missiles and 476 drones.
AFP reporters met several Kyiv residents outside an apartment building largely destroyed in the attack.
"Half the building has been destroyed. The roof is gone," said 32-year-old factory worker Sabina Mambetova, standing outside the rubble of her home in the eastern Darnytskyi district.
"I've been left without an apartment, alone with my child. I don't know what to do now."
Some 52,000 people, including 4,500 children packed into underground stations to shelter from the barrage – the highest number in recent years, according to the Kyiv metro.
Others hunkered down in basements or corridors through the night as blasts shook buildings across the city.
"It's hard," 32-year-old doctor Kateryna Kucheryava told AFP from the metro as the attack was unfolding. "My child is used to sleeping in complete silence and darkness.
"I picked her up and carried her down. She woke up and now she's not sleeping anymore."
Along station platforms, locals set up tents and lay on air mattresses and camping chairs, while mothers tried to sleep clutching babies to their chests.
- ZELENSKYY CUTS SHORT VISIT -
The EU's top diplomat Kaja Kallas said she would propose new sanctions on Moscow over the attack.
The attack came hours after Zelenskyy cut short a visit to Dublin on Wednesday, citing intelligence reports of an impending Russian strike.
Zelenskyy said Russian President Vladimir Putin "has been preparing this massive strike against Ukraine for some time now."
Ukraine has stepped up long-range drone attacks inside Russia in recent weeks, targeting energy infrastructure and military targets.
Russian officials have reported repeated strikes in border regions, while Moscow has said its air defences have intercepted hundreds of drones from Ukraine in recent days.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Flowers and a raised fist: Teheran's Grand Mosalla readies for Khamenei farewell
Article:
Teheran: At the entrance to the vast religious complex where Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's body is set to lie in state from Saturday, dozens were hard at work under an intense heatwave to prepare for the slain supreme leader's grand funeral.
Security was on high alert ahead of the start of the funerary activities for Iran's ruler of three decades, with dozens of personnel stationed at the main entrance to the Grand Mosalla, methodically stopping each car in the vicinity.
Passengers must present a special permit to enter the premises, which have yet to be opened to the public.
AFP obtained special access on Thursday, as a handful of curious bystanders watched the preparations from just outside the complex.
Within, the walls were draped in massive portraits of the late Khamenei, alongside black flags of mourning and red ones symbolising martyrdom and vengeance.
In one image, Khamenei – then the president – appears alongside young fighters in the Iran-Iraq war of the 1980s.
Hossein Moghadassi, one of those who has been working on the site for days, said "we are planting flowers and watering the shrubs for the farewell ceremony of our martyred guide."
He was clad in a hat and a scarf to cover his face as temperatures soared, with trucks transporting hundreds of boxes of drinking water in anticipation of the mercury surpassing 35 degrees Celsius (95 degrees Fahrenheit) on Saturday, the first of six days of national mourning.
"People will come from all over Iran. There will be huge crowds," Moghadassi said.
- 'VENGEANCE' -
Authorities expect between 15 and 20 million people to participate in the funeral in Teheran alone.
The gates to the Grand Mosalla are set to open at 6am (0230 GMT) on Saturday.
Within the premises, dozens of ambulances and rescue vehicles have been parked in preparation for the funeral of the supreme leader, who was killed in US-Israeli strikes on Feb 28.
Black banners bear several of Khamenei's most famous quotes, while an image of the late leader with a raised fist – a symbol of resistance against the West – was ubiquitous across the site.
Other banners read: "We are mourning, but we remain on our feet."
The Mosalla's main building, a mosque, will host the supreme leader's remains over three days for "pilgrims", according to the organisers.
On Monday, a procession will travel through the streets of the capital before arriving on Tuesday in the Shia holy city of Qom.
Khamenei will be buried on July 9 in Mashhad in northeastern Iran, another holy city and the late ruler's birthplace.
Iran's top negotiator in talks with the US, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, on Thursday called for a massive turnout as a way to avenge Khamenei's death.
"I invite all the Iranian people... to write a glorious page in the history of Islamic Iran through your presence," said Ghalibaf, who is also Iran's parliament speaker.
"The nation's call for vengeance must ring in the ears of the whole world."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother claims son was killed for refusing to join secret society [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  A 22-year-old youth from Kulaijaya, Johor was allegedly killed after refusing to associate himself with a secret society gang in his area, his mother said.
R. Arun Kumar was hacked to death on May 21. Seven suspects were charged with his murder last month.
The victim's family, however, are still not at peace as the remaining members of the gang are still on the loose.
Breaking her silence for the first time since the murder, Arun's mother, K. Devi, said her son was brutally killed for not wanting to join a secret society gang.
The 52-year-old told the New Straits Times that she was opening up now since the suspects have already been charged.
Arun was the leader of an Urumi Melam group, which used to play traditional music at temple and social events.
Devi, a housewife, said the family believed Arun had been targeted because he refused to associate with or join the gang despite repeated attempts to recruit young men in the area.
Arun had several musicians under his care and the gang assumed that if he had joined them, the others would follow suit.
"He never got involved in other people's business and yet he somehow became a target of the gang," said Devi.
Arun, affectionately known as "AK Boy", was the youngest of four siblings.
"Although he was not formally employed, he earned a living by managing his own Urumi Melam troupe, performing traditional Indian percussion music. He and a few other boys around the same age were in this Urumi Melam group."
His family is now living in fear as several suspects believed to be involved in the case are still around.
"We have been told there are still others who have yet to be arrested. As long as they remain free, we will never have peace of mind," said Devi.
Recounting what happened on the day her son was killed, she said Arun told her he was going out to settle some payments for his members and would be home early.
"We only found out what had happened after relatives called us at about 3am."
Devi described Arun as a quiet, respectful and caring  son who always put his family first.
"His death has destroyed all the dreams we had together.
"We are still struggling to come to terms with his murder. Nothing can bring our son back, but we want everyone responsible to be brought to justice."
She appealed to local leaders, particularly former Pekan Nanas assemblyman Tan Eng Meng, to address the growing influence of secret societies in the town.
Devi claimed that youths in the area were also increasingly being approached for recruitment by gangs.
"We hope the authorities will not take this case lightly. Please listen to the concerns of residents and work together with the police to rid Pekan Nanas of secret society activities.
"Don't wait until another life is lost before taking action."
Police arrested 10 suspects for the murder of Arun and charged seven of them.
The remaining three are expected to be prosecution witnesses in the trial.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Monthly adjustment system
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Consumers are now seeing changes in their electricity bills in line with changing energy consumption patterns, including hotter weather and the growing number of people working from home.
The changes are not arbitrary but are linked to a more transparent and equitable energy cost adjustment mechanism — the automatic fuel adjustment (AFA).
Previously, fuel cost adjustments were made through the imbalance cost pass-through  mechanism, which was reviewed every six months.
Importantly, 85 per cent of domestic consumers remain protected from AFAs  because their monthly electricity consumption is below 600kWh.
Only consumers whose monthly electricity consumption exceeds 600kWh, or whose monthly bills exceed RM216, are subject to charges such as the AFA, retail charge and the eight per cent Service Tax.
HOW AFA WORKS
The AFA is a monthly adjustment mechanism that factors in changes in global fuel prices, foreign exchange rates and actual generation costs to ensure the tariff system remains relevant to current energy market conditions.
The AFA rate, measured in sen per kWh, is determined based on verification by the Energy Commission or an official announcement following the minister's decision.
Importantly, the AFA is not a tariff increase but a monthly adjustment mechanism that may take the form of either a rebate or a surcharge, depending on market conditions.
Universiti Putra Malaysia, Putra Business School economist and lecturer Assoc Prof Dr Ahmed Razman Abdul Latiff said consumers should understand that the base tariff and the AFA are two separate components of the electricity pricing system.
He said the base tariff is fixed for a regulatory period and covers fixed costs such as infrastructure development, network operations and the basic cost of electricity supply, while the AFA is adjusted according to fluctuations in fuel prices and foreign exchange rates.
"An increase in electricity bill does not necessarily mean the base tariff has been raised, as it may also be influenced by higher electricity consumption or AFA adjustments reflecting changes in global energy costs."
For example, when fuel prices and foreign exchange rates decline, eligible consumers receive AFA rebates that reduce their electricity bills. When costs rise, surcharges are applied transparently.
IMPACT OF AFA
Since July 2025, the government has provided about RM2.7 billion in AFA rebates.
However, the  Middle East conflict has affected fuel prices, causing the AFA to shift from a rebate to a surcharge in May 2026.
Razman said that amid growing uncertainty in the global energy market due to geopolitical conflicts, supply chain disruptions and fluctuations in foreign exchange rates, mechanisms such as the AFA allow the tariff system to respond more quickly to actual cost changes.
"This approach reduces the risk of a mismatch between actual costs and the prices charged, while helping to maintain the financial stability of the energy sector and ensure a reliable electricity supply."
The growing trend of working from home has also changed daily electricity consumption patterns, making energy monitoring increasingly important.
WAYS TO REDUCE
ELECTRICITY BILLS
Consumers can take several practical steps to reduce their electricity bills more effectively:
1. MONITOR electricity usage and set a spending limit using the Energy Budget feature in the myTNB application.
2. USE electricity efficiently by setting air conditioners between 24°C and 26°C, choosing four- or five-star energy-efficient appliances, using washing machines only with full loads, and switching off appliances when not in use.
3. SWITCH to the Time-of-Use (ToU) scheme by shifting electricity consumption to off-peak hours to enjoy lower rates and reduce electricity bills.
4. CONSIDER installing a rooftop solar system at home.
With the implementation of the AFA, consumers are provided with greater transparency on the factors affecting their electricity bills.
By having access to better information and adopting more efficient energy consumption practices, they can better manage household expenses while supporting more sustainable energy use.
To learn how the AFA affects electricity bills and to check the latest monthly rates and three-month forecasts, visit the myTNB website www.mytnb.com.my/tariff.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UPNM developing rocket, missile tech modules
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Universiti Pertahanan Nasional Malaysia (UPNM) will make it compulsory next year for engineering students to take modules on missile and rocket technology, as part of efforts to develop Malaysia's defence expertise.
UPNM vice-chancellor Lieutenant-General Datuk Arman Rumaizi Ahmad said: "We want to produce experts among the new generation so that when this industry grows, it is led entirely by local youths, thus, safeguarding the sovereignty of the country's defence assets without having to depend on or be dominated by external parties."
He said this during a recent working visit to Canberra.
Arman said the initiative was backed by strategic partnerships with  Australian institutions that have expertise in the field, including the University of Southern Queensland (UniSQ) and the University of New South Wales.
Initial discussions have been held with UniSQ and a delegation from the institution will visit UPNM soon to refine how the module will fit into existing engineering programmes.
The strategy to develop local expertise comprises four main phases: skills enhancement through short courses for lecturers and workers, syllabus coordination for undergraduate and postgraduate students, collaboration with industry players, and industrial placements for practical exposure.
Industry partners in the initiative include the Weststar Group and Rocket Technologies International.
Arman added that the Defence Ministry had entrusted UPNM to be the main coordinator in pooling expertise from other public universities, such as Universiti Teknologi Mara, in line with the National Defence Industry Policy launched in January.
"This is a new strategic approach. Although it will not happen overnight, UPNM is committed to taking concrete steps to ensure that Malaysia can  build its own rockets in the future." — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TNB to plant 25,000 mangroves
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
